summary: Jeff Atwood argues that effective software development requires a balance between planning and coding, criticizing both extremes. He observes that real-world implementation constantly invalidates or transforms initial designs, making extensive upfront planning wasteful. The post specifically targets 'Software Architects' who create UML diagrams without writing code, calling this approach borderline laziness and a recipe for disaster. Atwood emphasizes that design is inherently messy and iterative, citing research showing even top designers use trial-and-error processes. His core message is that developers must prototype in code to make informed decisions rather than planning in abstract isolation.
key_points: Implementation and design have a tight feedback loop - plans made without code are often thrown away or altered beyond recognition
Over-planning is just as futile as no planning; software development is a 'wicked problem' requiring iterative approaches
The 'Software Architect' role that only produces UML diagrams without coding is criticized as lazy and dangerous
Design is inherently messy and trial-and-error even for top designers - initial solutions are quite likely to be wrong
Developers should make decisions based on experience and data gathered from actual code prototypes
money_quotes: Good programmers get off their butts.
Coding without planning is just as futile as coding with too much planning.
UML is great if you don't want to do any work; you just draw pictures of what it would look like if work was actually done.
You can't architect a real world application on a whiteboard.
When you're using Photoshop as a design tool, all things are possible. Visual Studio, unfortunately, isn't that forgiving.
Far from being a predictable, structurable, routinizable process, design is a messy, trial-and-error thing.
The initial design solution is quite likely to be wrong.
key_insight: Real software architecture requires hands-on coding because design decisions made without implementation feedback are uninformed guesses that will inevitably be discarded or drastically altered.
